title: OPC's Growing Pains: From Monolithic Skill to Scalable Flow Architecture
summary: Heavy usage of /opc loop exposed critical bottlenecks in OPC's architecture: scattered flow definitions, missing test infrastructure, and excessive deferrals caused by locked plan hashes. Chronicles the architectural debates, plugin system trade-offs, and open-source strategy decisions that shaped the refactor.
body:
# OPC's Growing Pains: From Monolithic Skill to Scalable Flow Architecture

This problem had been brewing for months. OPC started as a simple skill—a slash command for Claude Code that spawned multiple virtual roles and orchestrated tasks through a DAG. It worked so well that more and more of my work gravitated toward `/opc loop`. dream-works runs on OPC, ink-flow runs on OPC, even Memra's build process uses OPC loops.

Then the cracks started showing.

## The "defer to next loop" epidemic

The first symptom was OPC loops constantly producing "defer to next loop" results. A loop was designed to complete a set of planned tasks in a single iteration, but in practice, most tasks kept getting postponed. The root cause was specific: OPC loop plans get hash-locked during the `init-loop` phase and can't be modified mid-execution. If a task's prerequisites aren't met in the current loop (waiting for user confirmation, external API responses, etc.), that task has no choice but to defer.

This design originally ensured loop determinism—after each loop, you knew exactly "what got done, what didn't." But as task graphs grew complex (dozens of tasks, multiple dependency chains), the defer ratio kept climbing. What should have been single-loop operations now required multiple rounds to complete.

## The testing void

The second symptom was worse: OPC had virtually no test coverage. Yes, a tool designed to drive testing in other projects had no tests itself. I discovered this irony firsthand one evening when I had OPC run Playwright screenshot validation on a project. It worked, but the baseline for image comparison was the previous run's screenshot, not a committed reference image. In other words, as long as each iteration "looked about right," OPC would report green. This wasn't visual regression testing—this was "LLM glances and shrugs" testing.

Claude and I had a lengthy philosophical debate about testing strategy. The core question: what should tests actually validate for OPC as a skill (essentially a prompt + orchestration logic)?

My position: 1) CLI-level E2E tests (given input, verify output structure); 2) UI testing (if OPC generates HTML, use Playwright for screenshot comparison); 3) All test cases should persist with the project, not get discarded after runs. Claude argued for adding baseline management—screenshot baselines need to be committed to repos, with pixel diff thresholds and formal baseline update processes.

The final approach maintains a `.opc/` directory in each repo using OPC, storing baselines, test fixtures, and OPC artifacts. This content travels with projects, not people (unlike memex, which follows users—a crucial distinction).

## Architecture debates: Orchestrator vs Flow boundaries

After solving the testing issue, deeper architectural problems surfaced. OPC was simultaneously playing two roles: 1) generic multi-role task orchestration engine; 2) domain-specific workflow container (dream-works as one flow, ink-flow as another). This code mixing meant every OPC core change risked breaking some specific flow.

I launched `/opc` to have several architect roles discuss this problem. The core disagreement centered on:

**Option A**: Split OPC into core + plugins. Core handles role management, DAG orchestration, loop control. Each domain flow (dream-works, ink-flow) maintains independence as a plugin.

**Option B**: Keep OPC monolithic but add a flow definition layer. Domain flows get defined through configuration files (not code), with OPC core loading different flows by reading configs.

**Option C**: More radical layering—open-source OPC core, keep domain flows as proprietary extensions in private repos.

I initially favored A, then got convinced to blend B and C. The reasoning: plugin systems carry too much maintenance overhead. Each plugin needs its own lifecycle (install, update, uninstall), version compatibility management, and API stability guarantees. For a tool with essentially one user (myself), this overhead isn't justified.

Final architecture:
- Open-source OPC core stays in `~/.claude/skills/opc/`
- Flow definitions go in `~/.claude/skills/opc/flows/`, split between native (general flows shipped with OPC) and extended (user-defined domain flows)
- dream-works, ink-flow become extended flows, with code in private repos, deployed by copying to flows/extended/ directory

This approach avoids plugin system complexity while maintaining clear code boundaries. OPC core changes won't affect extended flows, and vice versa. The only coupling point is the flow definition schema—as long as the schema stays stable, both sides can iterate independently.

## Open-source strategy considerations

With the architecture settled, one question remained: how much should open-source OPC include?

My position: open-source OPC core + native flows, keep extended flows private. This isn't a technical decision (the code itself isn't secretive) but a business one—dream-works flows contain my domain knowledge for specific areas, which represents competitive advantage. Open-sourcing the core engine helps others build their own flows, but shouldn't give away my domain flows too.

One architect role challenged this during discussion: "If OPC's value lies in flows rather than the engine, open-sourcing just the engine means giving people an empty shell." Fair point, but I believe the engine has independent value—DAG-based multi-role orchestration solves a non-trivial problem. Even without pre-built domain flows, users can leverage the OPC engine to construct their own workflows.

## Implementation

After architectural discussions came implementation. This part was relatively mechanical: refactoring directory structure, changing flow definitions from hardcoded to configuration-based loading, adding `.opc/` directory initialization logic, updating configurations in `~/.claude/`.

The final step was `npm publish` + `git push`. By session end, changes were live. Two regrets remained: 1) the loop defer issue only got mitigated (allowing some tasks to reschedule after plan updates), not eliminated; 2) the testing framework got built, but specific test cases still need gradual addition through future usage.

This OPC refactor wasn't the kind of exciting 0-to-1 work that gets people energized. It was more like doing major chassis work on a car that's been running for six months—it'll still run without the work, but gets bumpier over time. The changes won't show immediate visible improvements, but will make all OPC-dependent workflows more stable long-term.

That's probably the essence of infrastructure work: no applause when you fix it, everyone complains when you don't.
